claim: A loop is an automated prompt that runs on a schedule, on a hook, or until a goal is met—not a new paradigm but a formalization of automation patterns that existed before AI
actionable: Think of loops as jobs you'd give to employees: heartbeat (every N minutes), cron (at specific times), hook (on events), or goal (until outcome is achieved)
source_title: How to write AI agent loops in Claude Code and Codex

claim: Four loop types exist: heartbeat (regular intervals), cron (scheduled times), hook (event-triggered), and goal loops (run until outcome is achieved)—each serves different automation needs
actionable: Choose heartbeat for polling tasks, cron for scheduled jobs, hooks for event reactions, and goal loops for target-driven work
source_title: How to write AI agent loops in Claude Code and Codex

claim: Effective loops need five foundational components: work trees (isolated workspaces), skills (repeatable procedures), plugins/connectors (tool access), sub-agents (delegated work), and state tracking (to-do lists)
actionable: Set up all five before deploying a loop; each prevents conflicts and enables reliable automation
source_title: How to write AI agent loops in Claude Code and Codex

claim: Goal loops are harder to write than scheduled loops because they require precise success criteria; if the goal is vague, agents burn tokens indefinitely until context fills or timeout occurs
actionable: Write goal-loop criteria as specific measurable outcomes; test with short timeouts first
source_title: How to write AI agent loops in Claude Code and Codex

claim: Loops can spawn sub-loops: a parent loop can kick off child loops (heartbeat, cron, goal) to delegate specialized work, creating hierarchical automation
actionable: Design agent hierarchies where a parent routine spawns sub-agents; each sub-agent can have its own loop type
source_title: How to write AI agent loops in Claude Code and Codex

claim: Loops risk becoming expensive if validation criteria are too thin or goals are under-specified—always monitor loop cost and set explicit token limits or turn constraints
actionable: Track loop execution costs; set max iteration counts and token budgets before deploying to production
source_title: How to write AI agent loops in Claude Code and Codex

claim: Slack/message connectors in loops enable agent output to trigger human-in-the-loop workflows; agents can alert teams about status changes or ask for approval
actionable: Wire message connectors (Slack, email, Discord) into loop outputs to keep humans informed without constant polling
source_title: How to write AI agent loops in Claude Code and Codex

claim: Scheduled routines in Claude CoWork (morning briefing example) are loops—users with scheduled tasks are already using agentic automation even if they don't call it loops
actionable: Recognize that features you already use (scheduled tasks, automation) are loops—build on that mental model
source_title: How to write AI agent loops in Claude Code and Codex

claim: GitHub Actions provide a natural integration point for code-review loops in Claude Code; agents can monitor PRs, run checks, and provide feedback autonomously
actionable: Trigger loops via GitHub Actions (webhooks on PR creation, commits); agents can review code without manual intervention
source_title: How to write AI agent loops in Claude Code and Codex

claim: Loop validation should use explicit success criteria checked by sub-agents; overly complex or missing validation causes tokens to burn without producing usable output
actionable: Write a specialized validation sub-agent with a clear success metric; make it checkable and binary where possible
source_title: How to write AI agent loops in Claude Code and Codex
